enrichment.short_description: Open source, CNCF-graduated distributed tracing platform originally built at Uber to monitor and debug microservices.
enrichment.seo_title: Jaeger Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jaeger is a free, open source distributed tracing platform born at Uber and graduated by the CNCF. See features, architecture, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jaeger is a free, open source, end-to-end distributed tracing platform used to monitor and troubleshoot transactions in complex microservices architectures. It was created inside Uber Technologies, where engineer Yuri Shkuro, a co-founder of the OpenTracing project and author of the book 'Mastering Distributed Tracing', led its development; the first commit landed in Uber's internal repository on August 3, 2015. The name comes from the German word for hunter, an allusion to hunting down the root cause of latency and errors as a request hops across dozens or hundreds of services. Uber open sourced Jaeger in 2017 and donated it to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) on September 13, 2017 as an incubating project; it reached the top tier of Graduated maturity on October 31, 2019, alongside Kubernetes and Prometheus, making it one of the earliest and most widely adopted distributed tracing systems in the cloud native ecosystem. Jaeger is not a company or a paid product but a community-governed open source project under the CNCF (part of the Linux Foundation), so it has no headquarters, employee count, or venture funding in the traditional sense; it is sustained by contributions and sponsorship from organizations including Uber, Google, GitHub, Grafana Labs, Red Hat, and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. Architecturally, Jaeger was inspired by Google's Dapper paper and Twitter's Zipkin, and it has since realigned itself around OpenTelemetry, the CNCF's vendor-neutral instrumentation standard: modern Jaeger (Jaeger v2) is built on top of the OpenTelemetry Collector framework and natively ingests trace data over the OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP), while still supporting legacy Jaeger and Zipkin formats for backward compatibility. It supports pluggable storage backends, including Cassandra, Elasticsearch and OpenSearch, Kafka as a buffering layer, and, as of release 2.18.0, native ClickHouse support for high-volume, cost-efficient trace storage at scale. Core features include a web UI that visualizes individual traces as waterfall and flame graphs, a service dependency graph that shows how microservices call one another, root cause and latency bottleneck analysis, adaptive and probabilistic sampling strategies to control data volume in high-traffic systems, and context propagation across dozens of language SDKs via OpenTelemetry instrumentation libraries for Go, Java, Python, Node.js, C++, and more. Because it is 100 percent open source under the Apache 2.0 license, Jaeger has no pricing tiers of its own: teams self-host the collector, query service, and storage backend, typically on Kubernetes, and pay only for their own infrastructure and, optionally, third-party commercial support. This makes it the default choice for engineering teams who want distributed tracing without vendor lock-in or per-host or per-trace billing, and it is commonly paired with Prometheus for metrics and a log aggregator such as Loki or the ELK stack to form a complete observability stack. Typical users are platform, SRE, and backend engineering teams running microservices on Kubernetes who need to debug latency spikes, understand service dependencies, or perform capacity planning across distributed systems, and its use extends far beyond Uber into thousands of organizations embracing cloud native architectures. Its closest open source alternative is Zipkin, an earlier and architecturally similar tracing system that Jaeger has largely superseded in new deployments, and Grafana Tempo, a newer OpenTelemetry-native tracing backend that competes on simplicity and tight integration with the Grafana stack. On the commercial side, Jaeger competes indirectly with fully managed application performance monitoring products such as Datadog APM, New Relic, Honeycomb, Lightstep, and Grafana Cloud Traces, which trade self-hosting effort for a hosted experience and added analytics at the cost of usage-based pricing. Jaeger's differentiation is that it remains free, self-hosted, standards-based, and backed by open governance rather than a single vendor, making it attractive to organizations that prioritize cost control, data ownership, and long-term neutrality over turnkey convenience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jaeger is an open source distributed tracing system that helps engineering teams understand how requests flow through microservices architectures. Originally built at Uber in 2015 to solve the company's own scaling pains, it was open sourced in 2017 and donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation, where it graduated to top-tier project status in 2019 alongside Kubernetes and Prometheus.

The project has since evolved to align tightly with OpenTelemetry, the industry standard for instrumentation, meaning Jaeger now ingests OTLP trace data natively and can be deployed as an OpenTelemetry Collector-based backend. It remains entirely free and self-hosted, run on infrastructure the adopting team controls rather than a vendor's cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jaeger's UI lets engineers drill into a single trace as a waterfall or flame graph, showing exactly which service call added latency or threw an error, alongside a service dependency graph that maps how microservices call one another across the whole system.

Under the hood it supports pluggable storage backends (Cassandra, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Kafka, and now ClickHouse), adaptive sampling to control data volume at high traffic, and broad language coverage through OpenTelemetry SDKs for Go, Java, Python, Node.js, C++, and more.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jaeger itself has no pricing: it is licensed under Apache 2.0 and free to run in any environment. Costs come entirely from the infrastructure a team provisions to run the collector, query service, and chosen storage backend, plus optional third-party commercial support.

For teams that don't want to operate Jaeger themselves, several vendors offer managed OpenTelemetry-compatible tracing backends (such as Grafana Cloud Traces) that are wire-compatible with Jaeger's data model, letting organizations start self-hosted and migrate to a managed option later without re-instrumenting their code.

features.title: Trace visualization
features.description: Waterfall and flame-graph views break down exactly where time is spent across a request's path through multiple services.

features.title: Service dependency graphs
features.description: Automatically generated maps show which services call which, useful for onboarding and impact analysis.

features.title: OpenTelemetry native
features.description: Jaeger v2 is built on the OpenTelemetry Collector and accepts OTLP data directly, alongside legacy Jaeger and Zipkin formats.

features.title: Pluggable storage
features.description: Supports Cassandra, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Kafka, and native ClickHouse for scalable, cost-efficient trace storage.

features.title: Adaptive sampling
features.description: Probabilistic and adaptive sampling strategies keep data volumes manageable in high-throughput production systems.

features.title: Root cause analysis
features.description: Correlates spans across services to pinpoint the origin of latency spikes and failures.

features.title: Broad SDK ecosystem
features.description: Instrumentation available for Go, Java, Python, Node.js, C++, and other languages via OpenTelemetry.

features.title: Kubernetes-friendly deployment
features.description: Ships with Helm charts and an operator pattern for running Jaeger natively on Kubernetes clusters.

pros: Completely free and open source with no usage-based billing
Graduated CNCF project with strong governance and long-term stability
Deep OpenTelemetry alignment future-proofs instrumentation investments
Flexible storage backend choices to match existing infrastructure
Large community and wide adoption across cloud native companies
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing operational maintenance
No built-in alerting or anomaly detection out of the box
Storage backend tuning (Cassandra/Elasticsearch) can be operationally complex at scale
UI is functional but less polished than commercial APM dashboards
No official managed cloud offering directly from the project

faqs.question: Is Jaeger free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jaeger is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no license fees or usage caps; you only pay for the infrastructure you run it on.

faqs.question: Who created Jaeger?
faqs.answer: Jaeger was created at Uber Technologies, led by engineer Yuri Shkuro, and was open sourced in 2017 before being donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation.

faqs.question: Does Jaeger support OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jaeger v2 is built on the OpenTelemetry Collector framework and natively accepts trace data via the OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP).

faqs.question: What storage backends does Jaeger support?
faqs.answer: Jaeger supports Cassandra, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Kafka as a buffering layer, and, since version 2.18.0, native ClickHouse.

faqs.question: Is Jaeger a managed cloud service?
faqs.answer: No. Jaeger is self-hosted software; there is no official managed cloud version, though several vendors offer OTLP-compatible managed tracing backends.

faqs.question: What is Jaeger commonly used with?
faqs.answer: It is typically paired with Prometheus for metrics and a log aggregator such as Loki or the ELK stack to build a complete observability stack for microservices.

use_cases.title: Microservices latency debugging
use_cases.description: Engineers trace a slow request end-to-end to identify which specific service or downstream call is responsible for added latency.

use_cases.title: Service dependency mapping
use_cases.description: Teams use Jaeger's dependency graphs to understand and document how dozens of microservices interact before major refactors or migrations.

use_cases.title: Root cause analysis during incidents
use_cases.description: During an outage, on-call engineers use trace data to quickly narrow down which service introduced errors or timeouts.

features: Community support via GitHub and Slack

enrichment.short_description: Publicly traded Apple device management platform providing MDM, identity, and endpoint security for Mac, iPhone, and iPad fleets.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for first 3 devices, then $4/device/month (Jamf Now)
enrichment.seo_title: Jamf Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jamf is the leading Apple device management platform for businesses and schools. Compare Jamf Pro, Jamf Now, and Jamf Protect pricing, features and rivals.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jamf is an Apple-exclusive enterprise mobility management (MDM) and endpoint security company best known for Jamf Pro, the device management platform used to configure, secure, and support fleets of Mac, iPhone, iPad, and Apple TV devices in businesses, schools, and government agencies. The company was founded in 2002 in Eau Claire, Wisconsin by Zach Halmstad, Christopher Thon, and Chip Pearson, who launched its original flagship product, The Casper Suite, to solve Mac management problems that generic Windows-centric IT tools could not handle. The company's name is a nod to Laszlo Jamf, a fictional scientist character in Thomas Pynchon's novel 'Gravity's Rainbow'. Jamf has since relocated its headquarters to Minneapolis, Minnesota, and grew into a publicly traded company on NASDAQ under the ticker JAMF following its 2020 IPO, reporting roughly 2,595 employees and, as of its most recent disclosures, servicing tens of thousands of organizations managing tens of millions of Apple devices worldwide. In October 2025, Jamf agreed to be acquired by private equity firm Francisco Partners in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $2.2 billion, with the transaction completing on January 30, 2026; Jamf is expected to retain its name, product lines, and Minneapolis headquarters as it transitions to private ownership. Jamf's product suite spans several tiers built around Apple's native MDM framework. Jamf Pro (the successor to Casper Suite) is the enterprise-grade core, offering zero-touch deployment via Apple Business Manager, configuration profile management, patch and app deployment, inventory reporting, and workflow automation for large, complex device fleets. Jamf Now targets small businesses with fewer than 25 employees and offers a self-service, no-IT-department-required setup where the first three managed devices are free and additional devices are billed per device per month, with a 14-day free trial and no long-term contract required. Jamf Connect handles cloud identity and authentication, letting organizations bind Apple devices to identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, or Google Workspace without binding to a legacy directory. Jamf Protect delivers endpoint security and threat detection purpose-built for macOS and mobile, including malware protection, content filtering, and Zero Trust Network Access, and Jamf School serves K-12 and higher-education institutions with classroom management and student device controls. Jamf sells its core offerings bundled into two business-facing packages, Jamf for Mac and Jamf for Mobile, both combining Jamf Pro, Jamf Connect, and Jamf Protect under one subscription, priced on a custom, quote-based basis that industry estimates place between roughly $3.67 and $12.50 per device per month depending on device type, feature tier, and deployment scale; enterprise contracts commonly range from $30,000 to $100,000 or more annually depending on fleet size. Target customers range from IT departments managing a few dozen Macs to Fortune 500 enterprises and school districts managing tens of thousands of Apple devices, with particular strength in regulated and security-conscious sectors like healthcare, finance, and education. Jamf's most direct competitors are Mosyle, Kandji, and Addigy, all Apple-only MDM platforms that compete aggressively on price and simpler UX, along with cross-platform unified endpoint management suites like Microsoft Intune, VMware Workspace ONE, and Ivanti, which manage Apple devices alongside Windows and Android but with less Apple-specific depth. Jamf differentiates itself through the breadth and maturity of its Apple-specific feature set, deep day-one support for new Apple OS releases, its own threat intelligence and endpoint security stack purpose-built for macOS, and the scale of its enterprise deployment track record, which newer, cheaper Apple MDM entrants have not yet matched at the largest organizational scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jamf is the dominant platform for managing Apple devices in business and education environments, built entirely around macOS, iOS, iPadOS, and tvOS rather than trying to be a generic cross-platform MDM. It began life in 2002 in Eau Claire, Wisconsin as The Casper Suite and has grown into a publicly traded, Minneapolis-headquartered company serving tens of thousands of organizations.

In October 2025 Jamf agreed to be acquired by Francisco Partners for approximately $2.2 billion, with the deal closing January 30, 2026, moving the company from public to private ownership while it retains its brand and product lineup. Its core value proposition is deep, day-one compatibility with new Apple hardware and OS releases, something generalist MDM tools historically lag behind on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jamf Pro handles zero-touch enrollment through Apple Business Manager, configuration profiles, patch and application deployment, and detailed inventory and compliance reporting for large device fleets. Jamf Connect layers cloud identity on top, binding devices to providers like Okta or Azure AD without a traditional on-prem directory, and Jamf Protect adds macOS and mobile-specific threat detection, malware defense, and Zero Trust Network Access.

For smaller organizations, Jamf Now strips this down into a self-service dashboard usable without dedicated IT staff, while Jamf School adapts the same underlying management engine for classroom and student-device use cases in K-12 and higher education.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jamf does not publish transparent list pricing for its enterprise products; Jamf for Mac and Jamf for Mobile are sold as custom, quote-based subscriptions bundling Jamf Pro, Connect, and Protect, with industry estimates placing per-device costs roughly between $3.67 and $12.50 per month depending on scale and feature selection.

Jamf Now is the exception, with clear self-service pricing: the first three managed devices are free permanently, and additional devices are billed per device per month, with a 14-day free trial and no minimum commitment, making it accessible for small businesses that want to try Apple MDM without a sales negotiation.

features.title: Zero-touch deployment
features.description: Devices auto-enroll and configure themselves out of the box via integration with Apple Business Manager and Apple School Manager.

features.title: Configuration and patch management
features.description: Push OS updates, security patches, and configuration profiles across an entire Apple device fleet remotely.

features.title: App deployment
features.description: Distribute and manage both custom in-house apps and App Store apps at scale, including licensing.

features.title: Cloud identity (Jamf Connect)
features.description: Bind Apple devices to modern identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, or Google Workspace instead of legacy directory services.

features.title: Endpoint security (Jamf Protect)
features.description: macOS and mobile-native threat detection, malware protection, and Zero Trust Network Access.

features.title: Inventory and compliance reporting
features.description: Real-time visibility into device compliance, installed software, and security posture across the fleet.

features.title: Education-specific tools (Jamf School)
features.description: Classroom management, student device restrictions, and content filtering tailored for K-12 environments.

features.title: Self-service small business tier
features.description: Jamf Now offers an IT-department-free setup for organizations managing fewer than 25 devices.

pros: Deepest, most mature Apple-specific MDM feature set on the market
Fast day-one support for new macOS, iOS, and iPadOS releases
Integrated identity (Connect) and endpoint security (Protect) reduce tool sprawl
Scales from small businesses (Jamf Now) to Fortune 500 enterprises
Strong presence and purpose-built tooling in education (Jamf School)
cons: Enterprise pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Apple-only focus means a separate tool is needed for Windows or Android
Can be costly at scale compared to newer, leaner competitors
Feature depth adds a learning curve for smaller IT teams
Ongoing acquisition by Francisco Partners introduces some uncertainty about future roadmap direction

faqs.question: What is Jamf used for?
faqs.answer: Jamf is used to enroll, configure, secure, and support fleets of Apple devices, including Mac, iPhone, iPad, and Apple TV, in businesses, schools, and government agencies.

faqs.question: How much does Jamf cost?
faqs.answer: Enterprise pricing for Jamf Pro-based bundles is custom quoted, typically estimated between $3.67 and $12.50 per device per month; Jamf Now for small businesses is free for the first 3 devices, then billed per device per month.

faqs.question: Is Jamf only for Apple devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jamf is built exclusively around macOS, iOS, iPadOS, tvOS, and visionOS, though Jamf for Mobile also extends limited support to Android devices.

faqs.question: Who owns Jamf?
faqs.answer: Jamf was a publicly traded company on NASDAQ (JAMF) until Francisco Partners completed a $2.2 billion acquisition on January 30, 2026, taking it private.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Jamf Pro and Jamf Now?
faqs.answer: Jamf Pro is the enterprise-grade platform for large, complex device fleets with dedicated IT administration, while Jamf Now is a simplified, self-service tier aimed at small businesses with fewer than 25 employees.

faqs.question: Does Jamf offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Jamf Now offers a 14-day free trial and permanently free management of the first three devices; enterprise Jamf Pro trials are arranged through Jamf's sales team.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Apple fleet management
use_cases.description: Large organizations use Jamf Pro to zero-touch deploy, configure, patch, and secure thousands of Mac and iOS devices across departments.

use_cases.title: K-12 and higher-ed device management
use_cases.description: Schools use Jamf School to manage student iPads and Macs, enforce content filtering, and control classroom app access.

use_cases.title: Small business Apple onboarding
use_cases.description: Small companies use Jamf Now to set up and secure a handful of company Macs and iPhones without hiring dedicated IT staff.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and lean IT teams

enrichment.short_description: Free, open source, fully peer-to-peer encrypted messaging and calling app with no central server, backed by the FSF.
enrichment.seo_title: Jami Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jami is a free, open source, fully peer-to-peer encrypted messenger with no central server. See its history, features, and how it compares to Signal.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jami is a free, libre, and open source communication platform offering instant messaging, audio and video calling, group chats and conferences, and screen sharing, distinguished from nearly every mainstream messenger by its fully distributed, peer-to-peer architecture that requires no central server to relay conversations. It is developed by Savoir-faire Linux, a Montreal, Quebec-based open source consulting and software company, and its lineage traces back to December 2004, when Savoir-faire Linux launched the SFLphone project, an open source SIP softphone for Linux. SFLphone was renamed Ring in May 2015 as the project expanded beyond simple VoIP calling into a full peer-to-peer communications platform, and Ring was renamed again to Jami in December 2018 to avoid trademark confusion with other commercial products using the word 'Ring'. Jami is officially a GNU package, meaning it is endorsed and distributed under the umbrella of the GNU Project and backed by the Free Software Foundation (FSF); it won the FSF's Free Software Awards Social Benefit Award in 2023 in recognition of its privacy-preserving, decentralized design. The software is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 or later, and the full source code is publicly auditable. Architecturally, Jami uses a distributed hash table (OpenDHT, also created by the Jami team) for peer discovery instead of routing messages and calls through company-controlled servers; once two devices find each other via the DHT, all messaging, voice, and video traffic flows directly and end-to-end encrypted between the participants' devices, meaning Jami's maintainers never see, store, or have access to conversation content. Users on the same local network can even communicate without any internet connection at all. Because there is no central account server, creating a Jami identity requires no phone number, email address, or other personal information, and the platform is entirely free, ad-free, and free of feature or file-size limits. Key features include one-to-one and group text messaging with offline message queuing, audio and video calls, multi-party video conferences, screen sharing, SIP account compatibility (letting Jami interoperate with traditional telephony infrastructure), swarm-based multi-device conversation syncing, and a plugin/extension system for adding capabilities like call recording, blur backgrounds, or transcription. Jami runs natively on GNU/Linux, Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, Android TV, and via a web client, with the ability to link multiple devices to a single distributed identity. There is no company revenue model behind Jami beyond Savoir-faire Linux's broader consulting business and community/grant support; the software itself has no premium tier, subscription, or paid features. Its target audience includes privacy and security-conscious individuals, journalists, activists, and organizations wary of centralized surveillance, as well as GNU/Linux and free-software communities who value auditable, self-sovereign communication tools; it is also used in some enterprise and government contexts requiring communications that never transit a third party's servers. Jami's most direct competitors are other privacy-focused messengers: Signal, which is end-to-end encrypted but still relies on centralized servers operated by the Signal Foundation; Element, built on the federated (not fully peer-to-peer) Matrix protocol; Session, which uses onion-style routing through a decentralized network; Briar, which is peer-to-peer and Tor/Bluetooth-capable but text-focused and Android-only; and Tox, a similar peer-to-peer protocol with a smaller ecosystem. Jami differentiates itself by combining full peer-to-peer architecture (no servers at all in the data path) with a complete feature set spanning messaging, voice, video, conferencing, and SIP interoperability across all major desktop and mobile platforms, and by its formal status as an FSF-endorsed GNU package, giving it a level of free-software governance and philosophical alignment that most privacy messengers, including Signal, do not claim.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jami is a free and open source messaging and calling platform built around a genuinely peer-to-peer architecture: conversations travel directly between participants' devices rather than through a company's servers. It is developed by Savoir-faire Linux, a Montreal-based open source firm, and traces its roots to the SFLphone VoIP project launched in December 2004, later renamed Ring in 2015 and finally Jami in 2018.

As an official GNU package backed by the Free Software Foundation, Jami occupies a distinct niche among privacy messengers: rather than merely encrypting traffic that still passes through central servers (as Signal does), Jami eliminates the server from the data path entirely using a distributed hash table for peer discovery, so there is no company that could be compelled to hand over message content because none is ever stored centrally.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jami supports one-to-one and group text messaging, audio and video calls, multi-party video conferencing, and screen sharing, all end-to-end encrypted by default. It can also function as a standard SIP client, letting it interoperate with existing telephony systems, and its swarm-based conversation model keeps multiple linked devices in sync without a server acting as the source of truth.

Because no personal information is required to create an account, and communication can even happen over a local network without internet access, Jami is popular in privacy-sensitive contexts. Its extension system allows optional add-ons like call recording or background blur, and the app is available on GNU/Linux, Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Android TV.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jami is entirely free, with no subscription tiers, ads, or feature restrictions of any kind; every capability, including group video calls and file sharing, is available to all users at no cost. This reflects both its GNU/GPLv3 licensing and the fact that it has no central infrastructure to monetize, since calls and messages route directly between users' own devices.

Savoir-faire Linux, the company behind Jami, sustains the project through its broader open source consulting business, community contributions, and free-software grants and sponsorships rather than through Jami itself, which has no paid plan to compare against.

features.title: Peer-to-peer architecture
features.description: Messages and calls travel directly between devices using a distributed hash table for discovery, with no central relay server.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: All messaging, voice, and video traffic is encrypted end-to-end between participants by default.

features.title: Group messaging and conferencing
features.description: Supports group text chats and multi-party video conferences alongside one-to-one calls.

features.title: SIP interoperability
features.description: Can function as a SIP client, connecting to traditional telephony infrastructure alongside peer-to-peer contacts.

features.title: No account personal data required
features.description: Accounts are created without a phone number or email, using a distributed identity instead.

features.title: Offline and local-network messaging
features.description: Devices on the same local network can message and call each other without any internet connection.

features.title: Multi-device sync
features.description: Swarm-based conversations let a single identity be linked across multiple devices and stay in sync.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Native clients for GNU/Linux, Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Android TV.

pros: Completely free with no ads, limits, or paid tiers
True peer-to-peer design means no company holds message content
Backed by the Free Software Foundation as an official GNU package
Works across all major desktop and mobile platforms
Can operate entirely offline over a local network
cons: Smaller user base than mainstream messengers like Signal or WhatsApp makes adoption harder
Peer-to-peer calls can be less reliable than server-relayed calls behind restrictive NATs or firewalls
Interface and onboarding are less polished than commercial competitors
Group features and conferencing are less battle-tested at large scale
No official enterprise support contracts beyond community and Savoir-faire Linux consulting

faqs.question: Is Jami really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jami is completely free and open source with no subscription fees, ads, or feature limits.

faqs.question: Who develops Jami?
faqs.answer: Jami is developed by Savoir-faire Linux, a Montreal, Canada-based open source software and consulting company, and is an official GNU package backed by the Free Software Foundation.

faqs.question: Does Jami use a central server?
faqs.answer: No. Jami uses a distributed hash table for peer discovery and routes messages and calls directly between users' devices, so it has no central server storing conversation content.

faqs.question: What was Jami previously called?
faqs.answer: Jami began as SFLphone in December 2004, was renamed Ring in May 2015, and was renamed again to Jami in December 2018.

faqs.question: What platforms does Jami support?
faqs.answer: Jami is available on GNU/Linux, Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Android TV, with multi-device sync for a single account.

faqs.question: How is Jami different from Signal?
faqs.answer: Signal encrypts messages end-to-end but still routes them through centralized servers operated by the Signal Foundation, while Jami eliminates servers from the data path entirely using peer-to-peer connections.

use_cases.title: Private peer-to-peer team communication
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious organizations use Jami for internal messaging and calls that never transit a third-party server.

use_cases.title: SIP-compatible calling
use_cases.description: Users connect Jami to existing SIP telephony infrastructure for interoperable voice calling alongside peer-to-peer contacts.
use_cases.audience: IT teams with legacy VoIP systems

use_cases.title: Offline local-network messaging
use_cases.description: Teams or households on the same local network use Jami to message and call without needing internet access.

features: Unlimited messaging, audio, and video calls

enrichment.short_description: Open source, local-first ChatGPT alternative that runs open-weight AI models entirely offline on your own computer.
enrichment.seo_title: Jan Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jan is a free, open source ChatGPT alternative that runs AI models locally and offline. Compare its features, model support, and alternatives like LM Studio.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jan is a free, open source desktop application that provides a ChatGPT-like interface for running large language models entirely locally, on the user's own hardware, without requiring an internet connection or sending data to a third-party API. It is built and maintained by Menlo Research, a private company domiciled in Singapore and founded in 2023 by co-founders Daniel Ong and Nicole Zhu, both Stanford alumni, with Alan Dao leading AI research on the team; Menlo Research describes its mission around building 'open superintelligence' and, alongside Jan, publishes its own small open-weight models such as Jan-Nano, an agentic model optimized for local tool use. Jan's codebase, hosted under the janhq/jan and now menloresearch/jan GitHub organization, is released under the Apache 2.0 license, which permits commercial use, modification, and redistribution with attribution; the project has attracted more than 42,000 GitHub stars and over 5 million cumulative downloads across macOS, Windows, and Linux as of 2026, with an active Discord community exceeding 15,000 members. Technically, Jan is built as a Tauri-based desktop application with a TypeScript frontend, using llama.cpp as its inference backend, which allows it to run quantized versions of open-weight models efficiently on consumer CPUs and GPUs. Users can browse and download models directly from an in-app catalog spanning families like Meta's Llama, Alibaba's Qwen, Google's Gemma, Mistral, DeepSeek, and OpenAI's open-weight GPT-OSS models, with over 120 models available via Hugging Face integration. Beyond fully local operation, Jan is flexible enough to also connect to hosted cloud model APIs, including OpenAI's ChatGPT, Anthropic's Claude, and Google's Gemini, letting users mix local privacy-first inference with cloud model quality on a bring-your-own-API-key basis inside the same interface. A key feature for developers is that Jan can run as a local, OpenAI-API-compatible server, so any application or script built against the OpenAI API format can point at Jan instead and use locally hosted models as a drop-in replacement, without code changes. Additional features include chat history and organization, adjustable model parameters (temperature, context length, system prompts), extensions for customizing behavior, and a roadmap item for persistent memory across conversations. Because everything can run fully offline with no telemetry by default, Jan's target audience includes privacy-conscious individuals, developers who want to prototype against local models before deploying to production APIs, researchers experimenting with open-weight models, and organizations in regulated or air-gapped environments that cannot send data to external AI vendors. Jan is entirely free, with no subscription tiers, seat licenses, or paid features; Menlo Research does not currently monetize Jan directly, instead focusing on open source adoption, its own research models, and community growth, consistent with its GitHub-native, community-funded development model. Jan's closest competitors are LM Studio, a popular closed-source but free local-model runner with a similarly polished chat UI; GPT4All, an earlier open source local LLM chat client; Ollama, a widely used but more CLI/API-centric local model runner that developers often pair with separate UIs; and Msty, another local-first chat client. Jan differentiates itself by combining a fully open source codebase, a built-in model discovery and download hub, an OpenAI-compatible local API server for developers, and native support for mixing local and cloud models inside one interface, positioning it as both a consumer-friendly ChatGPT alternative and a developer tool for building on top of local inference.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jan is an open source desktop application that lets anyone run AI chat models locally on their own computer, offering a ChatGPT-style interface without requiring cloud access or an API subscription. It is built by Menlo Research, a Singapore company founded in 2023 by Stanford alumni Daniel Ong and Nicole Zhu, with the project quickly growing to over 42,000 GitHub stars and 5 million downloads.

Under the hood, Jan is a Tauri and TypeScript desktop app powered by the llama.cpp inference engine, which lets it run quantized open-weight models efficiently on ordinary consumer hardware. It ships under the Apache 2.0 license, meaning the full source is auditable and free to adapt, including for commercial use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jan includes an in-app model catalog for downloading open-weight models like Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral, DeepSeek, and GPT-OSS, with over 120 models accessible via Hugging Face integration, and it works entirely offline with no telemetry by default. Users can also connect it to cloud APIs such as OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, or Google Gemini using their own API keys, mixing local and cloud inference inside one chat interface.

For developers, Jan can run as a local server that mimics the OpenAI API format, so existing applications built against OpenAI's API can point at Jan and use local models as a drop-in substitute with no code rewrite, which has made it popular for prototyping and privacy-sensitive development workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jan is completely free and open source, with no subscription tiers, seat-based pricing, or feature paywalls; every capability, including the model catalog, local API server, and cloud model integrations, is available at no cost.

The only costs a user might incur are indirect: sufficiently powerful hardware to run larger local models comfortably, or usage fees charged by third-party providers like OpenAI or Anthropic if the user chooses to connect their own API key for cloud models within Jan.

features.title: Fully offline local inference
features.description: Runs open-weight models entirely on-device via llama.cpp, with no internet connection or data transmission required.

features.title: In-app model catalog
features.description: Browse and download models from families like Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral, and DeepSeek directly inside the app.

features.title: OpenAI-compatible local API server
features.description: Exposes local models through an OpenAI-format API so existing tools and scripts can use Jan as a drop-in backend.

features.title: Cloud model integration
features.description: Optionally connect OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, or Google Gemini using your own API key alongside local models.

features.title: Hugging Face integration
features.description: Access more than 120 additional models directly from Hugging Face's model hub.

features.title: Adjustable model parameters
features.description: Fine-tune temperature, context length, and system prompts per conversation or model.

features.title: No telemetry by default
features.description: Designed with privacy as a default, not an opt-out, for fully local usage.

features.title: Cross-platform desktop app
features.description: Available natively for macOS, Windows, and Linux.

pros: Completely free and open source under Apache 2.0
Works fully offline with no data leaving the device
OpenAI-compatible API makes it a drop-in local backend for developers
Supports both local open-weight models and cloud models in one app
Large, active community with frequent updates
cons: Local model quality and speed depend heavily on the user's own hardware
Larger, more capable models require significant RAM or GPU resources
Persistent memory across conversations is on the roadmap but not yet available
Less polished enterprise support compared to commercial AI platforms
Model management and quantization concepts have a learning curve for non-technical users

faqs.question: Is Jan free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jan is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no subscription fees.

faqs.question: Do I need an internet connection to use Jan?
faqs.answer: No. Jan can run entirely offline using locally downloaded open-weight models; an internet connection is only needed if you choose to connect a cloud model API.

faqs.question: Who makes Jan?
faqs.answer: Jan is built by Menlo Research, a Singapore-based company founded in 2023 by Daniel Ong and Nicole Zhu.

faqs.question: What models does Jan support?
faqs.answer: Jan supports open-weight models including Llama, Qwen, Gemma, Mistral, and DeepSeek locally, plus cloud models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini via API key.

faqs.question: Can developers build on top of Jan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jan can run as a local server exposing an OpenAI-compatible API, letting developers point existing applications at local models with no code changes.

faqs.question: What platforms does Jan run on?
faqs.answer: Jan is available as a native desktop application for macOS, Windows, and Linux.

use_cases.title: Local development and prototyping

Connect OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google Gemini API keys
Mix local and cloud models in one interface

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based clinic management and EHR software for health and wellness practitioners, offering booking, charting, and billing.
enrichment.seo_title: Jane App Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jane App is a Canadian clinic management and EHR platform for health and wellness practitioners. Compare Jane's plans, pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Jane App, commonly known as Jane, is a cloud-based practice management and electronic health record platform built specifically for health and wellness practitioners, including physiotherapists, chiropractors, massage therapists, counselors, naturopaths, and multi-disciplinary clinics. The company was founded by Ali Taylor and Trevor Johnston in North Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, where it remains headquartered today. The origin story traces back to 2011, when Ali Taylor opened Canopy Integrated Health, a multi-disciplinary clinic, and could not find scheduling and charting software flexible enough to serve multiple practitioner types under one roof; Trevor Johnston, who ran a marketing agency at the time, built the original tool to solve that problem. In 2012 the pair decided to turn the internal tool into a licensable product, and Jane officially launched to outside customers in 2014. Jane has since grown into a substantial company, with employee counts reported between roughly 500 and 1,000 depending on the source and date, serving clinics across Canada, the United States, and other markets. The platform's core offering centers on customizable, client-friendly online booking, flexible multi-practitioner scheduling, fully customizable SOAP-note and charting templates suited to different clinical disciplines, insurance billing and claims management, and integrated payment processing so clinics can handle intake, scheduling, treatment documentation, and payment in a single system. More recent additions include 1:1 and group telehealth appointments, an AI Scribe feature that automatically drafts clinical notes from appointment conversations, automated waitlist management, patient self check-in kiosks, secure messaging and automated email/SMS reminders, timesheets and staff task management, and Jane Websites, a bundled website-building add-on for clinics that want an integrated public-facing site. Jane prices its software in Canadian dollars across three core plans: Balance at CAD $54 per month, aimed at solo practitioners and capped at 20 appointments per month; Practice at CAD $79 per month, which removes the appointment cap and adds unlimited practitioner profiles, unlimited SMS reminders, and staff task management; and Thrive at CAD $99 per month, the top tier, which adds room and equipment scheduling, automated patient self check-in, packages and membership billing, and ratings/reviews and analytics integrations. Adding practitioners beyond the first costs roughly CAD $35 to $79.50 per month each depending on part-time or full-time status, and notable add-ons include unlimited AI Scribe notes for CAD $15 per practitioner per month (5 free notes per month are included on every base plan), group telehealth for CAD $15 per practitioner per month, insurance billing for CAD $20 per month plus small per-staff fees, and Jane Websites for CAD $59 per month per clinic. All plans include unlimited human customer support, custom-branded online booking, secure messaging, intake forms and treatment plans, and 1:1 telehealth. Jane commonly offers a free trial period, frequently promoted as one month free with no credit card required, to let clinics evaluate the platform before committing. Jane's target market is small to mid-sized health and wellness practices, particularly multi-disciplinary clinics that need one system flexible enough to serve several types of practitioners, distinguishing it from many US-centric EHR platforms built around a single specialty. Its main competitors include SimplePractice, a US-focused practice management tool popular with mental health and therapy practices; Cliniko, an Australian-founded competitor with similar multi-discipline clinic features; TheraNest and Practice Better, which lean toward mental health and coaching niches; and WebPT, which specializes specifically in physical therapy. Jane differentiates itself through its clean, modern user interface, native support for a wide range of allied health disciplines within one platform, Canadian data residency options, transparent published pricing (unusual in the category), and a product philosophy built around reducing administrative burden for practitioners rather than maximizing billing codes for insurance-heavy US markets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jane App is a cloud-based clinic management and EHR platform built for health and wellness practitioners such as physiotherapists, massage therapists, chiropractors, and counselors. It was co-founded by Ali Taylor and Trevor Johnston in North Vancouver, Canada, after Taylor struggled to find flexible enough software for her own multi-disciplinary clinic, Canopy Integrated Health, in 2011; the tool Johnston built for her became a commercial product in 2012 and launched publicly in 2014.

Jane has grown from a single-clinic solution into a company with several hundred employees serving practices across Canada, the United States, and beyond, while staying focused on its original mission of supporting multi-disciplinary practices with one flexible system rather than specialty-specific software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Jane combines client-friendly online booking, flexible scheduling across multiple practitioners and rooms, fully customizable charting and SOAP-note templates, insurance billing, and integrated payment processing in a single platform. Newer additions include telehealth (both 1:1 and group), an AI Scribe that drafts clinical notes automatically, automated waitlists, and patient self check-in.

Administrative tools like staff timesheets, task management, secure messaging, and automated appointment reminders round out the platform, and clinics can add on Jane Websites for an integrated public-facing site, reducing the number of separate tools a practice needs to run day-to-day operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Jane publishes transparent pricing in Canadian dollars across three plans: Balance at CAD $54 per month for solo practitioners with a 20-appointment cap, Practice at CAD $79 per month with unlimited appointments, and Thrive at CAD $99 per month adding automation, self check-in, and membership billing. Additional practitioners cost roughly CAD $35 to $79.50 per month each.

Add-ons extend the core plans, including unlimited AI Scribe notes at CAD $15 per practitioner per month, group telehealth at CAD $15 per practitioner per month, insurance billing at CAD $20 per month, and Jane Websites at CAD $59 per month. Jane frequently offers a free trial, commonly one month, so clinics can test the platform before subscribing.

features.description: Timesheets and task management tools for clinic administrative staff.

Supports many allied health disciplines natively in one platform
Modern, clean interface that is easy for both staff and patients to use
Built-in AI Scribe reduces charting time for practitioners
Free trial available with no credit card required
cons: Pricing is listed in Canadian dollars, adding currency conversion friction for some markets
Additional practitioners and add-ons can meaningfully increase monthly cost
Balance plan's 20-appointment cap may be limiting for busier solo practices
Some advanced features (self check-in, memberships) are reserved for the top Thrive tier
Insurance billing depth may not match specialty-specific US platforms for complex claims

faqs.question: What is Jane App used for?
faqs.answer: Jane App is practice management and EHR software used by health and wellness practitioners to handle online booking, scheduling, charting, telehealth, billing, and payments.

faqs.question: How much does Jane App cost?
faqs.answer: Jane's core plans start at CAD $54 per month (Balance), with Practice at CAD $79 per month and Thrive at CAD $99 per month, plus per-practitioner and add-on fees.

faqs.question: Who founded Jane App?
faqs.answer: Jane App was founded by Ali Taylor and Trevor Johnston in North Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, launching commercially in 2014.

faqs.question: Does Jane App offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jane commonly offers a free trial period, often one month, with no credit card required to start.

faqs.question: What types of practitioners use Jane App?
faqs.answer: Jane is used by physiotherapists, chiropractors, massage therapists, counselors, naturopaths, and other allied health and wellness practitioners, especially multi-disciplinary clinics.

faqs.question: Does Jane App include telehealth?
faqs.answer: Yes, all plans include 1:1 telehealth appointments, with group telehealth available as a paid add-on.

enrichment.short_description: Open source, general-purpose WebRTC gateway server by Meetecho for building custom video, audio, and streaming applications.
enrichment.seo_title: Janus Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Janus is a free, open source WebRTC gateway from Meetecho used to build video conferencing, streaming, and SIP applications. See features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Janus is a free, open source, general-purpose WebRTC server, often described as a WebRTC gateway, that developers use to build custom real-time audio, video, and data applications such as video conferencing systems, live streaming platforms, webinar tools, and SIP-to-WebRTC bridges. It is developed and maintained by Meetecho s.r.l., a company founded in 2009 as an academic spin-off of the University of Naples Federico II in Naples, Italy, where it remains headquartered. Meetecho was co-founded by Alessandro Amirante, Lorenzo Miniero, and Tobia Castaldi; Lorenzo Miniero is the chairman of Meetecho and the principal author and lead maintainer of the Janus project, while Alessandro Amirante serves as the company's CTO. Since its founding, Meetecho has focused on real-time multimedia communication technology, beginning with VoIP and academic conferencing tools before shifting attention to WebRTC as the standard matured; Janus was built and released as Meetecho's flagship open source contribution to that ecosystem. Architecturally, Janus is written in C and designed with a deliberately small footprint and a modular, plugin-based architecture: the Janus core handles the low-level WebRTC plumbing, including ICE negotiation, DTLS handshakes, SRTP encryption, and RTP/RTCP media relay, communicating with browsers and other WebRTC clients over a JSON-based signaling protocol, while all application-specific logic lives in separate server-side plugins that developers load on top of the core. Official plugins bundled with Janus include an EchoTest for validating WebRTC connectivity, a VideoRoom plugin implementing a full multi-party video conferencing room (a selective forwarding unit, or SFU), an AudioBridge plugin for audio-only mixing, a Streaming plugin for one-to-many live broadcast, a Recordplay plugin for recording and replaying WebRTC sessions, and a SIP plugin that bridges WebRTC clients to traditional SIP telephony infrastructure, letting a browser place and receive calls through a standard SIP provider. Because Janus exposes a low-level, general-purpose API rather than a fully opinionated product, it is used as an infrastructure building block by other companies and open source projects rather than as an end-user application; developers write their own client-side and often server-side logic around the Janus core to assemble a finished conferencing, streaming, or telephony product. Janus is released under the GNU General Public License and is entirely free to self-host, with no licensing fees, usage caps, or paid tiers for the core software itself; Meetecho generates revenue separately by offering commercial consulting, custom plugin development, and support contracts to organizations building production systems on top of Janus. Its typical users are software engineers and companies building real-time communication features into their own products, ranging from telehealth platforms and online education tools to live-event streaming services and custom video conferencing products, who need lower-level control over WebRTC media handling than a fully managed SDK provides. Janus's most direct open source competitors are mediasoup, a Node.js-based SFU library with a similarly low-level, developer-first design; Kurento, an older WebRTC media server with a heavier media-processing pipeline; Jitsi Videobridge, the SFU underlying the Jitsi Meet conferencing product; and Pion, a WebRTC implementation in Go used to build custom media servers from lower-level primitives. On the commercial, fully managed side, Janus indirectly competes with hosted video infrastructure platforms like LiveKit, Twilio Video, Agora, and Daily, which trade the flexibility and cost savings of self-hosting for usage-based pricing and a managed, higher-level API. Janus differentiates itself through its long track record (in production since the early 2010s), its plugin architecture that gives developers granular control over exactly what functionality they need, its native SIP gateway capability that most competing SFUs lack out of the box, and the availability of direct commercial support from Meetecho, the team that authored and continues to actively maintain the project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Janus is a free, open source WebRTC gateway server that gives developers low-level building blocks for real-time audio, video, and data communication rather than a finished conferencing product. It was created by Meetecho, an Italian company founded in 2009 as a spin-off of the University of Naples Federico II, and is led technically by Lorenzo Miniero, Meetecho's chairman and Janus's principal author.

Rather than shipping as an opinionated video conferencing app, Janus positions itself as infrastructure: developers build their own client applications and server logic around Janus's core WebRTC handling, using its modular plugin system to add exactly the functionality (conferencing, streaming, SIP, recording) that a given product needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Janus core, written in C, handles ICE negotiation, DTLS/SRTP encryption, and RTP/RTCP media relay, exposing a JSON-based signaling API that browsers and other WebRTC clients use to talk to the server. Application behavior is implemented through official plugins: VideoRoom for multi-party video conferencing, AudioBridge for audio mixing, Streaming for one-to-many broadcast, Recordplay for session recording and playback, and SIP for bridging WebRTC clients into traditional telephony.

This plugin-based design gives Janus a small footprint that can run on modest hardware for lightweight deployments, while still scaling up to full cloud-based video infrastructure for larger production systems, making it a popular choice for teams that want fine-grained control over their real-time media stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Janus itself is entirely free and open source under the GNU General Public License, with no cost to download, self-host, or modify the software, and no usage-based fees regardless of scale.

Meetecho, the company behind Janus, generates revenue not from the software itself but from separate commercial services: consulting, custom plugin development, and support contracts for organizations that want expert help deploying and operating Janus in production.

features.title: Modular plugin architecture
features.description: Core WebRTC handling is separated from application logic, which lives in swappable server-side plugins.

features.title: SIP gateway
features.description: Bridges WebRTC clients to traditional SIP telephony infrastructure for interoperable voice calling.

features.title: AudioBridge
features.description: Mixes audio from multiple participants for audio-only conferencing use cases.

features.title: Streaming plugin
features.description: Supports one-to-many live broadcast scenarios such as webinars or live events.

features.title: Recording and playback
features.description: Recordplay plugin captures and replays WebRTC sessions for later review.

features.title: Small footprint
features.description: Designed to run efficiently on modest hardware as well as scale to large cloud deployments.

features.title: JSON signaling API
features.description: Simple, well-documented JSON protocol for browsers and clients to communicate with the server.

pros: Completely free and open source with no usage-based fees
Modular plugin system gives developers granular control over functionality
Native SIP gateway support that many competing SFUs lack
Long production track record and active maintenance by its original authors
Commercial support available directly from Meetecho when needed
cons: Requires significant developer effort to build a finished product on top of it
No official managed cloud hosting option from Meetecho
Lower-level API means a steeper learning curve than fully managed video SDKs
Documentation, while solid, assumes real-time media and networking expertise
Scaling and operating in production requires WebRTC infrastructure knowledge

faqs.question: Is Janus free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Janus is free and open source under the GNU General Public License, with no licensing fees or usage caps.

faqs.question: Who created Janus?
faqs.answer: Janus was created by Meetecho, an Italian company founded in 2009 by Alessandro Amirante, Lorenzo Miniero, and Tobia Castaldi, with Miniero as its principal author.

faqs.question: What is Janus used for?
faqs.answer: Janus is used to build custom real-time video conferencing, live streaming, webinar, and SIP-to-WebRTC bridging applications.

faqs.question: Is Janus a finished conferencing app?
faqs.answer: No, Janus is a low-level WebRTC gateway and infrastructure component; developers build their own client and server logic on top of it using its plugin system.

faqs.question: Does Janus support SIP calling?
faqs.answer: Yes, its official SIP plugin bridges WebRTC clients to traditional SIP telephony infrastructure.

faqs.question: Can I get commercial support for Janus?
faqs.answer: Yes, Meetecho, the company behind Janus, offers commercial consulting, plugin development, and support contracts.
